title: Making ‘First Reformed,’ the Best Movie of the Year So Far, With Paul Schrader | The Big Picture (Ep. 66)
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Paul Schrader about First Reformed, the slow cinema that shaped it, and the career disaster that sent him back to the quiet movie he'd sworn off.

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: Ethan Hawke as Reverend Toller, a Schrader man consumed by ecological despair and a crisis of faith. Schrader chose the Ordet ending over the Diary of a Country Priest one.

films.title: Taxi Driver
films.note: Schrader's screenplay. His editor pointed out how much Travis Bickle had crept into First Reformed, calling it the 'obsessive glue' holding the borrowed religious influences together.

films.title: Diary of a Country Priest
films.note: Bresson. One of the withholding models Schrader reread; its priest-falls-out-of-frame ending was the one he almost used.

films.title: Ordet
films.note: Dreyer. Kent Jones nudged him toward its resurrection ending, a response to a miracle that's carnal rather than pious.

films.title: Silent Light
films.note: Carlos Reygadas. Cited among the newer slow-cinema films Schrader studied.

films.title: Ida
films.note: Paweł Pawlikowski's film about a nun; the conversation after Schrader gave Pawlikowski an award set him on the road to First Reformed.

films.title: Dying of the Light
films.note: Taken away from him and recut by others, the debacle that pushed him toward alcoholism and, later, insisting on final cut. He re-edited his own version as 'Dark,' which he says can't be shown.

films.title: Dog Eat Dog
films.note: The Nic Cage film where he first used final cut, a loud, outrageous experiment. First Reformed used that same freedom in reverse, staying quiet instead of cutting loose.

films.title: The Canyons
films.note: The crowdfunded DIY movie with Bret Easton Ellis and Lindsay Lohan. Lesson learned: never again.

films.title: The Death of Stalin
films.note: His pick for the last great film he'd seen; hadn't known Armando Iannucci's work before.

films.title: Raging Bull
films.note: Schrader screenplay, named in the intro.

films.title: The Last Temptation of Christ
films.note: Schrader screenplay for Scorsese.

films.title: American Gigolo
films.note: Among the films he directed, named in the intro.

films.title: Cat People
films.note: Schrader's directed remake, named in the intro.

films.title: Affliction
films.note: Screening alongside Light Sleeper at his retrospective that night.

films.title: Hardcore
films.note: Cited as a film he doesn't rewatch or dwell on while working.

films.title: Light Sleeper
films.note: The other film in his retrospective that evening.

films.title: Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters
films.note: Boots Riley told him he lifted an elevator shot from it straight for his own Sundance film.

films.title: A Man Escaped
films.note: Bresson, one of the transcendental-style touchstones behind the film.

films.title: Pickpocket
films.note: Bresson, named among the films he went back to.

films.title: Journey to Italy
films.note: Rossellini's 'Voyage in Italy,' cited among his influences.

films.title: I Confess
films.note: Hitchcock. Montgomery Clift's suffering man of the cloth was the physiognomy Schrader wanted, which led him to Ethan Hawke.

films.title: Zabriskie Point
films.note: Antonioni. The exploding-house, slow-motion-debris ending was one of three finales Schrader considered, ruled out for cost.

films.title: Collateral
films.note: Michael Mann. Schrader almost skipped it, then saw it recommended around his Facebook cinephile circle and loved it.

Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters.title: Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters
